skill.name: using-git-worktrees
skill.description: Guides creation of isolated Git worktrees with directory selection, .gitignore checks, setup commands, and clean baseline verification.
skill.summary: Create isolated Git worktrees for feature work without disturbing the current checkout.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static command execution findings are true positives because the skill instructs agents to run Git, package manager, build, and test commands. The hidden file and weak cryptography findings are false positives or low-risk context; no malicious intent, network exfiltration, credential access, or prompt injection was found.

medium_findings.title: Repository Command Execution Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run Git, package manager, build, and test commands. This is expected for a worktree setup skill, but package install and test commands can execute repository-defined scripts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and semantically required by the skill. Risk depends on the repository being operated on, so this is elevated but not evidence of malicious skill intent.

medium_findings.title: Automated Repository Mutation
medium_findings.description: The skill directs agents to update .gitignore and commit that change when a worktree directory is missing from ignore rules. This can alter repository history without a separate confirmation step.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file modification and commit instruction is directly stated. It is legitimate workflow guidance, but it creates a governance risk in repositories requiring review before commits.

low_findings.title: Hidden Directory References Are Benign
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged .worktrees and ~/.config paths. These are normal local workspace locations for Git worktrees and do not show secret harvesting or hidden persistence behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are documented as workspace storage choices and are not combined with credential access or network transfer. This makes the filesystem alerts strong false positives.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptographic algorithms at the skill description and integration section. No cryptographic operation or hashing command appears in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain prose about the skill and another skill name. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA1, or any cryptographic API use in the file.

low_findings.title: Shell Substitution Is Used For Local Project Detection
low_findings.description: The command substitution derives the repository root basename. It is local project inspection, not remote execution or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The substitution wraps git rev-parse and basename for a local directory name. The use is transparent and does not process untrusted remote input.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Manager Install Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup section recommends npm install, cargo build, pip install, poetry install, and go mod download. These commands may run project dependency hooks or download third-party code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly listed and can execute repository or dependency behavior. This is a known operational risk for developer automation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Git Worktree Creation With User-Selected Branch Name
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow builds a path and branch from variables before running git worktree add. Quoting reduces shell injection risk, but branch name validation is not documented.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The variables are quoted, so direct shell injection evidence is weak. The missing validation still merits review because branch names and paths affect filesystem operations.

content.value_statement: Developers need isolated branches without disrupting current work. This skill guides directory selection, safety checks, setup, and baseline verification.
actual_capabilities: Selects a worktree directory using existing folders, CLAUDE.md guidance, or user choice.
Checks .gitignore before creating project-local worktree directories.
Creates a Git worktree with a new branch and changes into that workspace.
Detects common project types and runs matching setup commands.
Runs baseline tests and reports whether the new worktree is ready.
Documents common mistakes and required safety checks for worktree use.
limitations: It does not validate branch names before using them in Git commands.
Setup commands may execute repository-defined scripts or dependency hooks.
It assumes Git is installed and the current directory is inside a repository.
It does not manage cleanup after work is complete.

use_cases.title: Prepare Feature Work
use_cases.description: Create a separate workspace for a new feature while keeping the current checkout unchanged.

use_cases.title: Run Implementation Plans Safely
use_cases.description: Start planned implementation in an isolated worktree before making code changes.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Branch Setup
use_cases.description: Apply consistent worktree directory rules, ignore checks, dependency setup, and baseline tests.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Worktree
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new branch
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create an isolated Git worktree for feature/login and run the normal setup checks.

prompt_templates.title: Respect Project Conventions
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up a worktree for bugfix/cache using the repository preferred location from existing folders or CLAUDE.md.

prompt_templates.title: Verify Baseline Before Coding
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a worktree for refactor/api-client, install dependencies, run tests, and report any baseline failures before editing code.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare AI Implementation Workspace
prompt_templates.prompt: Before executing the approved implementation plan, create an isolated worktree, verify .gitignore safety, run setup, and summarize readiness risks.

output_examples.output: The assistant selects the preferred worktree directory, verifies ignore rules, creates the branch workspace, runs setup, and reports readiness.

output_examples.input: Set up a worktree, but tests fail during baseline verification.
output_examples.output: The assistant reports the failing baseline tests and asks whether to investigate or continue with known failures.

output_examples.input: No worktree directory exists in the project.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks project guidance, then asks the user to choose between a project-local or global worktree location.

best_practices: Confirm project-local worktree directories are ignored before creating them.
Run baseline setup and tests before starting implementation work.
Use explicit branch names and record the final worktree location.
anti_patterns: Creating worktrees inside the repository without checking ignore rules.
Running implementation work after baseline tests fail without user approval.
Assuming a worktree directory when the repository has no convention.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It guides an assistant through creating and validating an isolated Git worktree for development work.

faq.question: Does it create branches?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow uses Git worktree creation with a new branch name supplied during the task.

faq.question: Why does it check .gitignore?
faq.answer: Project-local worktree folders should be ignored so nested worktree contents are not accidentally committed.

faq.question: Can it run dependency installation?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends common setup commands for Node.js, Rust, Python, and Go projects.

faq.question: What happens if tests fail?
faq.answer: The workflow says to report failures and ask whether to proceed or investigate first.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. It can run repository commands and modify .gitignore, so review actions before execution.
